_readme: T-1.2 network-fixture matrix. This file holds ONLY the parameters; the set of
query shapes is derived by reading the code and lives in scripts/fixtures/record.mjs,
each shape carrying a file:line citation back to its call site.
To extend coverage (e.g. T-1.1 adding a station/date pair), edit this file and
re-run `yarn fixtures:record`. The recorder is idempotent: unchanged URLs are
re-fetched and rewritten, and index.json records what was captured when.
_dates_note: `primary` is what VITE_PINNED_DATE should be set to when running the app offline.
It is deliberately inside the ERA5-Land reanalysis lag (~5-10 days) so that the
Open-Meteo preliminary branch (api.ts:363, api.ts:466) is the one exercised on a
default page load — that branch is the main source of day-to-day non-determinism
The remaining dates are the T-1.1 (station, date) matrix, folded in now so that
ticket does not need a second recording pass.

dates.purpose: Pinned 'today'. One day before the T-1.2 recording date, so it falls inside the ERA5-Land reanalysis lag: climate-si has no `daily` row, and the page falls through to the live Open-Meteo forecast (api.ts:363 national, api.ts:466 per-station) and renders as preliminary. Freezing this is the whole point of the ticket.

Mid-summer date far enough in the past that ERA5-Land reanalysis is final, so the datasette-authoritative branch (api.ts:460) is exercised and Open-Meteo is never called. The counterpart to the primary date.

Leap day. month=2 day=29 is the case most likely to be missing from precomputed per-day tables (daily_window, annual_trend), because a doy-indexed pipeline built on a 365-day year has nowhere to put it. 2024 is the most recent leap year.
Day after the leap day, in a leap year. monthDayToDoy (api.ts:107-110) uses a fixed non-leap day table, so Mar 1 in a leap year is where a doy off-by-one would first show up.

Last day of February in a NON-leap year. The leap pair above only proves the Feb 29 -> Mar 1 transition; this is the control that shows what Feb 28 -> Mar 1 does when there is no Feb 29 in between. monthDayToDoy (api.ts:107-110) reads a fixed non-leap table, so doy(2,28) and doy(3,1) must be consecutive here and NOT consecutive in 2024 — an off-by-one that is invisible with only the leap pair recorded.

Mar 1 in a non-leap year, the counterpart to 2024-03-01. Same month/day, different year: the daily_window / annual_trend shapes are keyed on month+day and are therefore shared with the 2024 date (recorded once), but the date-keyed shapes (D5 daily station api.ts:457-459, D6 last7 api.ts:553-557) are distinct, and D6 is the one that matters — its 7-day lookback crosses Feb 28 rather than Feb 29.
Year boundary, low end. The +/-7-day climatology window (api.ts:56-61) has to wrap across the year end; Jan 1 is where wrap-around arithmetic breaks.
Year boundary, high end. Same wrap-around exposure as Jan 1, from the other side; also the last-7-days strip (api.ts:553-557) crosses into the previous year.

VERIFIED against the recorded fixtures, not assumed: Ljubljana 35.591 vs p5 19.35 / p95 31.36, and Kredarica 21.743 vs p5 5.99 / p95 16.79. Both are above p95, so this single date exercises the 'hell' branch (api.ts categorize) at the lowland station and the altitude station at once. August 2013 was a documented Slovenian heat episode.

VERIFIED: Ljubljana -5.759 vs p5 -4.24 — below p5. NOT an extreme at Kredarica: -14.707 against a p5 of -15.97 is inside the band, i.e. an ordinary early-February day up there. The February 2012 European cold wave was a lowland event in this dataset. The Kredarica p5- case is 1985-01-07 below; this date is kept for Ljubljana.

VERIFIED against the live datasette before recording, and the reason the 2012 date is not enough: Kredarica -23.707 vs p5 -16.76 (second-coldest day in its whole record, after 1968-01-13) and Ljubljana -11.759 vs p5 -5.34. Both below p5, so the 'freezing' branch is exercised at altitude — which 2012-02-07 does not do. January 1985 was a documented central-European cold wave.

_stations_note: All 18 ERA5 stations, as returned by stations.json (api.ts:292). Per-station
shapes that are cheap (one row, or one small table) are recorded for all of them.
`snapshot_stations` are the two T-1.1 names it over-samples: Ljubljana as the
lowland case and the page default (fetchMeta's default_location, api.ts:318), and
_variables_note: ALL FIVE options in the regression variable picker (RegressionPanel.tsx:10-16),
not just the temperature three. precipitation_sum and et0_evapotranspiration are
user-selectable from the same <select> (RegressionPanel.tsx:177-179) and reach
buildRegressionResult (api.ts:661) and fetchCalendar (api.ts:1378) with no filtering
on the way — so a run that omits them is one dropdown change away from a live
request. They are recorded for snapshot_stations, like the other non-default
Note the units differ: for these two the page labels the axis mm, not °C
(RegressionPanel.tsx:70 isPrecip). That is a display concern, not a fixture one —
_tropical_note: Slider bounds come from ERA5_CONFIGS (Era5TropicalChart.tsx:16 days 25-35,
Era5TropicalChart.tsx:29 nights 15-25); streak is the 1/2/3 select
(AliJeVroceERA5.tsx:198-200). The full 11x3 sweep is recorded only for
snapshot_stations; every other station gets the default threshold at streak 1,
which is what a default page load requests.
_derived_coverage: TWO QUERY FAMILIES CANNOT BE PLANNED FROM THIS FILE ALONE. Both were found by
T-1.1 on the first (station, date) case it ran, because neither is reachable on
a default page load. They are planned in scripts/fixtures/record.mjs instead.
D2b — the last-7 climatology lookback. fetchLast7 (api.ts:561-567) calls
fetchEra5WindowRow for the month/day of EVERY row the D6 query returns, so
selecting a station in the today card fires seven daily_window requests, six of
them for days that are not in the `dates` matrix. Those month/days are not
derivable arithmetically: outside the reanalysis lag they are the six preceding
calendar days, but ON THE PRIMARY DATE — which sits inside the lag by design —
the seven most recent rows are weeks earlier. record.mjs therefore fetches in two
passes and derives them from what D6 actually returned. T-1.2 missed this because
the page opens on the national average, for which fetchLast7 returns early
D8b — the doy round-trip. fetchRegression (api.ts:642) does not use the selected
date's month/day; it converts the analysis panel's doy back through the fixed
NON-LEAP table at api.ts:102. The two calendars disagree after Feb 28 of a leap
year, so selecting 2024-03-01 makes the regression panel and the hero cards query
MARCH 2. That is the T-4.5 defect, and the fixture set has to cover it for the
snapshot to freeze it. Derived arithmetically in record.mjs (doyRoundTrip).
_normalised: Recorded bodies are byte-for-byte what the server sent, with ONE exception.
Open-Meteo (shapes O1, O2) stamps every response with `generationtime_ms`, the
server's own timing for that request. It differs on every call even when the
forecast values are identical, so it alone made all 19 open-meteo/ files diff on
the 2026-07-22 re-record. That destroys the property that makes this fixture set
a control at all: re-running `yarn fixtures:record` against an unchanged upstream
must leave the working tree clean, so that a diff means THE DATA MOVED and not
THE RECORDER IS NOISY.
The field is therefore DELETED at record time (scripts/fixtures/record.mjs,
stripGenerationTime), not preserved and ignored. Safe because the app never reads
it: the only consumers of an Open-Meteo body are openMeteoMax (api.ts:255-256)
and openMeteoNationalMax (api.ts:274-277), and both read exactly
`daily.temperature_2m_max[0]`. Nothing else in code/ali-je-vroce-era5 mentions
`generationtime` (grep, 2026-07-22). Entries in index.json carry a `normalised`
marker.
Normalised responses are re-serialised with JSON.stringify, which also drops
whitespace and renormalises number literals — upstream `299.0` and `26.0` are
stored as `299` and `26`. Same values, and JSON.parse cannot tell the difference,
so the app sees identical numbers; but it does mean these 19 files are not a
byte-level record of the wire format. Verified 2026-07-22: two consecutive full
re-records produced ZERO diff across all 1258 files.
NOT normalised, and still diffs every run by design: index.json's `recorded_at`.
The byte-identity claim is about tests/fixtures/http/ only.
_uncapturable: VERIFIED BY MEASUREMENT, not assumption: with fixtures installed a default page
load makes 51 requests, of which 48 are same-origin. The 3 that still leave the
machine are listed below. NONE of them go through fetch(), which is why the shim
in code/ali-je-vroce-era5/fixtures/install.ts structurally cannot stop them — it
can only wrap fetch. Blocking these needs request interception at the browser
level (Playwright page.route), which is T-1.3's job.
1. fonts.googleapis.com — a <link rel=stylesheet> in the SITE-WIDE layout
(pages/_includes/base.html:9,12) plus an @import in styles/era5.css:9. Not an
ERA5 call at all; it affects every page on the site. Offline the browser falls
back to system fonts, which CHANGES TEXT METRICS — this is the one that will
make T-1.3 screenshots unstable if it is not blocked or self-hosted.
2/3. {a,b}.tile.opentopomap.org — Leaflet raster basemap tiles
(SeaLevelWidget.tsx:296), loaded as . Not recorded: the
tile set is unbounded (depends on Leaflet pan/zoom) and they are PNGs, not JSON.
Offline the basemap renders blank; the flood overlay, divider, legend and stats
still render, because those are same-origin assets (/data/flood-stats.json
SeaLevelWidget.tsx:312, /data/flood/*.png SeaLevelWidget.tsx:58).
The arso-si datasette (saGet, api.ts:43-47) and Vremenar (api.ts:236) are NOT
recorded because no UI path reaches them: fetchMeta never populates
arsoStationIds (api.ts:26) and builds station names from era5_name only
(api.ts:306-313), so no loc can start with 'arso:'. Slated for deletion in T-2.2
per D-2. Likewise the sidecar /api/live/tropical_* call (TropicalChart.tsx:253):
the component owning it is imported nowhere. T-2.3.
